# GitHub Copilot Instructions

## Project Overview
This is a Notes App built with Tauri, React, TypeScript, and Mantine UI components. The app allows users to create, view, and delete notes stored in a SQLite database. Features include a rich text editor for note content, modal-based note creation, and a clean list view sorted by most recent notes first. The main window maximizes on startup for optimal viewing.

## Tech Stack
- **Frontend**: React 18 + TypeScript + Vite
- **UI Library**: Mantine v7
- **Rich Text Editor**: Quill.js (react-quill) with all formatting features enabled
- **Icons**: @tabler/icons-react
- **Backend**: Rust (Tauri 2.x, edition 2024)
- **Database**: SQLite (rusqlite 0.33)
- **Build Tool**: Tauri CLI

## Project Structure
- `src/` - Frontend React/TypeScript code
  - `App.tsx` - Main application component with note CRUD operations
  - `main.tsx` - Entry point with Mantine provider
- `src-tauri/` - Rust backend code
  - `src/main.rs` - Tauri commands and database logic
  - `Cargo.toml` - Rust dependencies
  - `tauri.conf.json` - Tauri configuration
- `config/schema.sql` - Database schema definition
- `notes/notes.db` - SQLite database file (created at runtime relative to executable)
- `deploy/` - Deployment package folder (generated by build-all.bat)

## Build and Deployment
- `build-all.bat` - Complete build pipeline: frontend → Tauri → deployment package
- `deploy-app.bat` - Creates standalone deployment package in `deploy/` folder
- Deployment package includes: executable, database folder, installers (MSI/NSIS), README, and run script
- Database path is relative to executable location for portability

## Coding Conventions

### TypeScript/React
- Use functional components with hooks
- Use TypeScript for type safety
- Use async/await for asynchronous operations
- Use Mantine components for UI elements
- Handle errors with try-catch blocks and console.error

### Rust/Tauri
- Use `#[tauri::command]` for commands callable from frontend
- Return `Result<T, String>` for error handling
- Use rusqlite for SQLite database operations
- Store .db file in `notes/` subfolder
- Follow Rust naming conventions (snake_case for functions)
- Use edition 2024

## Common Patterns

### Frontend to Backend Communication
```typescript
const result = await invoke<ReturnType>('command_name', { param1, param2 });
```

### Modal-based Forms
- Use `useDisclosure` from @mantine/hooks for modal state management
- Modal dialogs use centered positioning and responsive sizing (75vh height for create forms)
- Rich text editor content is stored as HTML in the database

### Rich Text Editor
- Uses Quill.js (react-quill) with comprehensive toolbar configuration
- All formatting features enabled: headers, fonts, colors, lists, alignment, links, images, etc.
- Content stored as HTML in the database
- Plain text validation using regex to strip HTML tags
- Custom styling with border and border-radius for clean appearance

### Tauri Commands
```rust
#[tauri::command]
fn command_name(param1: Type1, state: State<AppState>) -> Result<ReturnType, String> {
    // Implementation
}
```

### Database Operations
- Use prepared statements to prevent SQL injection
- Handle database errors gracefully with `map_err`
- Return structured data as JSON-serializable types
- Use Mutex for thread-safe database access
- RFC3339 format for timestamps

## When Suggesting Code
- Prioritize type safety and error handling
- Use existing Mantine components for consistency
- Follow the established patterns in App.tsx and main.rs
- Ensure Tauri commands are properly registered in `invoke_handler`
- Validate inputs before creating notes (check for empty strings)
- Keep code concise and readable
- One method should do one thing only
- Method size should not exceed 50 lines
- Add 1-2 line JSDoc/Rust doc comments for each method
- Keep descriptions easy to understand
- Ensure code has no security vulnerabilities
- Use descriptive method names following conventions
- Use latest versions of libraries and tools
- please use function keyword instead of const, var etc. when creating top level functions in a file
- DO NOT create separate markdown/MD files after your prompt responses. Instead summaize the changes concisely in a paragraph or two in the Copilot chat window itself. Also update the copilot-instructions.md file with latest changes concisely so that subsequent prompts can use that for project context
